pages.text: The sky above Ahmedabad was filled with colors. Red, green, purple, yellow — kites danced and twirled like cheerful birds. 'Hold the string tight!' shouted <name> as her big red kite soared higher.
'Wow, <name>! Yours is the biggest!' said her friend Aarav. The wind whistled — whoooosh! — pulling the string tight. 'Up, up, up!' laughed <name>. But then — SNAP! The string broke!

'Oh no! My kite!' she cried. The red kite fluttered away, spinning wildly until — flap-flap! — it got stuck on the roof of the old post office building. 'I’ll get it back!' said <name> bravely. Aarav blinked. 'Are you sure? It’s really high!'

<name> nodded. 'It’s my lucky kite. I can’t let it fly away.' She climbed up the narrow stairs of her house, then jumped to the next rooftop. Thump! Step by step! The tiles were warm under her feet. A curious cat followed, meowing loudly.

'Shh, silly cat! You’ll scare the pigeons!' The wind blew stronger — whoooosh! — tugging at her dupatta. <name> balanced carefully on the edge. 'Just a little more…' she whispered. The cat meowed again.

Suddenly, a big gust of wind came — WHIRRR! The kite flapped dangerously close to the edge. <name> reached out — 'Got you!' she shouted, clutching the kite’s tail just in time. Thump! She grabbed the railing and pulled herself to safety. Her heart thudded — dhak-dhak!

Down below, Aarav cheered. 'You did it, <name>! You’re a kite hero!' <name> grinned, hugging the red kite close. 'Every kite deserves to fly again!' She tied the string tightly and let it lift once more into the sky.

The red kite danced proudly in the sky, its tail fluttering like a victory flag. The crowd clapped and whistled. Even the cat purred, curling by her feet. <name> laughed, eyes shining. 'Next year,' she said, 'I’m flying two kites!'
As the sun set behind the rooftops, her red kite glowed like a tiny piece of fire — fearless, free, and flying high.
